topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias walked through the familiar patch of woodland behind his childhood home, the sun dappling through the canopy in the way he remembered. He stopped dead, his breath catching in his throat. Where the sturdy oak, the one he’d painstakingly nurtured into a young sentinel when he was seven, should have stood, there was only a raw, jagged scar on the earth. The stump was ragged, freshly exposed, and the massive trunk was gone. Elias slowly lowered his hand, the calluses on his fingers suddenly feeling alien. He blinked, once, twice, trying to reconcile the vibrant, leafy memory with the stark reality before him. His jaw hung slack, his eyes wide and unblinking, fixed on the empty space where his youthful ambition had taken root. A low, involuntary sound escaped his chest, a choked whisper that seemed too loud in the quiet woods.

[story 2]

“No. No, this can’t be right.” Sarah muttered, her voice thin and reedy. She traced the disturbed soil with the tip of her boot, the earth feeling strangely loose and disturbed where the old maple used to anchor itself. It had been there, a towering promise in her youth, a landmark she’d pointed to as a kid. Now, there was just bare, churned ground, a gaping void where solid wood and shadow had once been. Sarah took a hesitant step back, bumping against a nearby bush, her hands flying up to grip her own arms. Her gaze darted frantically between the freshly cut debris and the distant line of the neighboring property, her breathing becoming shallow and quick. She stood frozen, her posture rigid, as if the sheer impossibility of the scene was pressing the air from her lungs.

[story 3]

I found it by accident, wandering down an overgrown path I hadn't taken in years. Then I saw it—the clearing where I’d planted that ridiculous, ambitious sapling as a child. The spot was barren. Not just cleared, but violently stripped. The remnants of the root system were haphazardly torn, like a careless vandal had raked through it. My mind refused to process the visual data. My feet stayed planted, rooted to the spot, unable to move toward the evidence. I just stared, my vision tunneling slightly, the colors of the surrounding foliage seeming muted, as if the world itself had momentarily stalled. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my stomach, heavy and undeniable, as I tried to force my eyes to register the devastation.
